coredata.dc:title: Visual monitoring of silver ions and cysteine using bi-ligand Eu-based metal organic framework as a reference signal: Color tonality
coredata.dc:creator: Sh. Mohammed Ameen, Sameera
Sher Mohammed, Nidhal M.
Omer, Khalid M.
coredata.dc:description: Multicolor visual sensing approach significantly improves accuracy, reproducibility and detection rate of targets. Herein, a novel red emitter bi-ligand Eu-based metal organic framework (BiL-Eu-MOF) was successfully synthesized using a one step solvothermal method and was evaluated as a reference signal for multicolor visual-based ratiometry detection. The synergistic effects offered by the double ligand and Eu significantly showed red shift excitation towards many cations and anions and fluorescence stability at least for 6 months. As a fluorescent multicolor-visual probe, selective blue emitter carbon dots to Ag+ ions were encapsulated into the pores of the red emissive of BiL-Eu-MOF to form blue-purple color. Addition of Ag+ ions changed emission color from blue-purple to red, while addition of cysteine (Cys) restored the original blue-purple color. Employing RGB analysis using smartphone, the detection mode showed good linearity of Ag+ and Cys in the range of 0–660 and 0–405 μM with limit of detection of 2.1 and 0.15 μM, respectively. Ultimately, our probe was applied for the quantification of silver ions in water samples and Cys in human serum samples showing excellent accuracy and precision.

head.ce:title: Visual monitoring of silver ions and cysteine using bi-ligand Eu-based metal organic framework as a reference signal: Color tonality

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multicolor visual sensing approach significantly improves accuracy, reproducibility and detection rate of targets. Herein, a novel red emitter bi-ligand Eu-based metal organic framework (BiL-Eu-MOF) was successfully synthesized using a one step solvothermal method and was evaluated as a reference signal for multicolor visual-based ratiometry detection. The synergistic effects offered by the double ligand and Eu significantly showed red shift excitation towards many cations and anions and fluorescence stability at least for 6 months. As a fluorescent multicolor-visual probe, selective blue emitter carbon dots to Ag+ ions were encapsulated into the pores of the red emissive of BiL-Eu-MOF to form blue-purple color. Addition of Ag+ ions changed emission color from blue-purple to red, while addition of cysteine (Cys) restored the original blue-purple color. Employing RGB analysis using smartphone, the detection mode showed good linearity of Ag+ and Cys in the range of 0–660 and 0–405 μM with limit of detection of 2.1 and 0.15 μM, respectively. Ultimately, our probe was applied for the quantification of silver ions in water samples and Cys in human serum samples showing excellent accuracy and precision.

ce:section.ce:para: Developing efficient visual method is very essential for clinical diagnosis and environmental analysis particularly in resource-limited settings [1,2]. Current bioanalysis trends are towards simplicity in operation, accuracy in the results, and non-toxicity precursors [3–5]. As a heavy metal ion, Ag+ is well known to play a significant role in the electrical, photographic, and pharmaceutical industry,hence, accurate monitoring of silver ions is a great of concern for public health and environmental analysis [6,7]. Cysteine (Cys), as a biothiol plays a critical role in protein synthesis, metabolism, and detoxification. Elevated Cys levels have been linked with neurotoxicity, and the deficiency of Cys is related with slow growth, skin lesions, hair depigmentation, weakness, liver damage, and edema [8–10]. Therefore, the detection of Ag+ and Cys levels with high sensitivity and selectivity has attracted much interest in recent years. Various methods have been applied for detection of Ag+ and Cys, such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [11], electrothermal atomic absorption spectrometry (ETAAS) [12], colorimetry [13–16] and fluorescent methods [17–23]. Although, some of these methods have acceptable sensitivity and selectivity, but they are costly and time-consuming. To overcome that, sensing methods using nanomaterials can be developed for the detection of Ag+ and Cys with high sensitivity, selectivity and low-cost.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are porous crystalline materials prepared by metal ions/clusters and organic ligands linking by coordination bonds [24–26]. During the last two decades, MOFs have been intensively investigated for biochemical applications due to their unique physicochemical properties [27–31]including porosity, high surface area, presence of cavities, high thermal stability, and functionality [32–36]. One approach to obtain desired functionalities in MOFs is via encapsulation with fluorescent nanomaterials inside the MOF pores. Thus, with appropriate and rational design, a new probe, luminescent material with MOFs will combine both properties of the precursors, or sometimes producing new unique properties [37,38]. Examples of the materials that can be inserted into the MOFs such as molecular fluorophores (dyes) [39–41], metal nanoparticles [42], metal oxides[43–46], quantum dots [47,48], and carbon nanodots [49–52].

The conventional MOFs includes reaction of one ligand (the linker) with one metal ions. However, there are more possibilities for preparation of MOFs using two different metal ions (bi-metal MOFs) or two different ligands (bi-ligand MOFs), that may alter optoelectronic, mechanical, structural properties of the new MOFs comparing to their counterparts [28–31]. Furthermore, moving from mono-MOFs to bi-MOFs can offer new properties and broaden the horizon of application of the MOFs. Bi-ligands MOFs are more interesting comparing to mono-ligand MOFs due to obtaining novel properties, such as high luminescence, modulating physical–chemical properties, additional functionalities, stability, catalytic activity, variety of architectures, pore size and shape tunability [53–58].

Recently, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted enormous research attentions compared to organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots. This is due to distinct optoelectronic characteristics, such as easy preparation, low-cost, ease of functionalization, biocompatibility, high photostability, and water solubility [59–68]. Smartphones detection-based fluorescence sensors are attracting intensive attention for point of care testing (POCT) in healthcare, food production, and environmental monitoring areas [69–76] for detection of ions[77,78], drugs [79–81], and biomolecules [82], biomarkers [83]. The common approaches used in fluorescence probes are either “turn-on” or “turn-off” fluorescence emission. Turn-off strategy relies on the quenching of color intensity under irradiation (same color), while turn-on mode depends on enhancing the color intensity. However, because human eyes have low resolution to distinguish decreasing of color intensity, color tonality is more preferred in visual detections [84,85]. In addition, the intensity of single emission is usually influenced by environmental factors, resulting in errors. To address this problem, it is rational to build ratiometric fluorescence, dual-signal fluorescence, either from one emitter or two emitters. The shortcomings of a single-emission fluorescence sensor can be solved by a ratiometric sensor based on the intensity ratio of two distinct emission centers. [86,87]. Compared with single emitting fluorescent probes, ratiometric fluorescent probes, the fluorescence intensity ratio at two well-resolved wavelengths to detect changes to target concentrations, and offer a built-in correction against external interferences. Thus, circumvent false signals origination from environmental effects and improve the signal accuracy and provide high sensitivity [88,89]. To this end, one should think about merging color tonality via ratiometry, visual mode using smartphone-based detection to be able to make a probe with ASSURED (Affordable, sensitive, specific, user-friendly, robust and rapid, equipment-free, and delivered to those who need them) criteria for point of care testing.

Herein, a novel ratiometry-based fluorescence probe was designed, in solution using smartphone-assisted visual mode. The synthesis of this color tonality probe is based on encapsulation of blue emitter carbon dots into pores of red emitter bi-ligand Eu-MOFs (Fig. 1 ). This bi-ligand Eu-MOFs exhibits no reactivity with common species in serum and water matrices. Thus, the bi-ligand MOF serves as an excellent internal reference signal. Ratiometry approach with visual mode using smartphone, color tonality, were designed and used for detection of silver ions in water samples and Cys in serum fluid successfully.

All materials and solvents were of analytical grades and used without purification. Europium (III) nitrate hexahydrate (99.9%) and 1,3,5-Benzene tricarboxylic acid (BTC, 99.4%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Sigma Aldrich, Germany). All other metal ions were of nitrate and chloride source obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. 1,10-phenanthroline (1,10-phen), silver nitrate (AgNO3), citric acid (CA), thiamine hydrochloride or vitamin B1 (VB1), NaOH, methanol, common carbohydrates such as: glucose (Glu), lactose (Lac), fructose (Fru), sucrose (Suc), common amino acids such as: histidine (His), tyrosine (Tyr), tryptophan (Trp), lysine (Lys), glycine (Gly), asparagine (Asp), glutathione (Glut), phenylalanine (Phe), serine (Ser), uric acid (UA), NaCl, KCl, Al (NO3)3, CoCl2, FeCl3, Cr (NO3)3, MgSO4, CdCl2, MnCl2, BaCl2, were all obtained from Alfa Aeser Germany. Dialysis membrane (molecular weight cut off, MWCO, 500 Da) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Deionized water was used for the preparation of the solutions. Ultrapure water was prepared with a Millipore system (18.2 MΩ cm).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken using transmission electron microscope (﻿TEC9G20, FEI, USA) at 200 KV. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) were measured on a scanning electron microscope combined with EDX (MIRA III, TESCAN, Czech). Powder X-ray diffraction patterns were recorded on X-ray diffractometer (PW1730, Netherland). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of the samples were measured on FTIR spectrophotometer (IRAFFinity-1, Shimadzu) in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was examined using thermogravimetric analyzer (Q600, TA, USA) scanning from room temperature to 900 °C under flowing nitrogen atmosphere. The fluorescent spectra were recorded on Fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary 60, Agilent, USA).

The CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method based on previously reported method with minor modifications [90]. In brief, 0.6 g of CA and 1.0 g of VB1 were dissolved in 25 mL double deionized water, then the mixture was stirred for 5 min to obtain a transparent solution. The pH of the mixture was adjusted with aqueous solution of NaOH (2 M) to make the medium neutral or alkaline. Then the solution was poured into a reactor, heated for 5 h at 160 °C and later cooled down at room temperature. To eliminate large particles a brownish suspension was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 30 min. Finally, the suspension was dialyzed against double deionized water through a dialysis membrane for 24 h. At last, the resulting product solution was stored at 4 °C for further use and characterization.

BiL-Eu-MOF was synthesized by one step solvothermal method referring to previously reported method with some modifications [91]. Briefly, Eu(NO3)3⋅6H2O (0.5 mmol), BTC (0.5 mmol), 1,10-phen (0.5 mmol) and NaOH (0.1 mmol) were dissolved in a 50% aqueous methanol solution (20 mL). The reaction mixture was stirred for 5 min and then poured into a reactor, heated for 2 days at 140 ◦C. The MOF obtained as white powders (under daylight) were washed with ultra-pure water for several times and dried at 60 °C in oven.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Ag+ and Cys
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent experiments were performed at room temperature selecting 280 nm as excitation wavelength. 5 mg BiL-Eu-MOFs and 0.05 mL CDs were dispersed in 10 mL ultra-pure water and stirred for 15 min to obtain a well-mixed CDs@BiL-Eu-MOF suspension. Then, various concentrations of Ag+ solution were added and mixed thoroughly, and the fluorescence spectra of the mixture solutions were scanned. For Cys detection, 5 mg BiL-Eu-MOF and 0.05 mL CDs were dispersed in 10 mL ultra-pure water and stirred for 15 min to obtain a well-mixed CDs@BiL-Eu-MOF suspension, followed by adding Ag+ solution (0.1 mM) to form the Purple-blue emission of CDs@BiL-Eu-MOF/Ag+. Later, different concentrations of Cys solution were added and mixed thoroughly, and then the fluorescence spectra of the mixture solution were scanned at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual assay for detection of Ag+ and Cys
ce:section.ce:para: The visual detection experiments were performed at room temperature selecting 365 nm as excitation wavelength. After 10 mg BiL-Eu-MOF in 10 mL deionized water, 0.4 mL of CDs was added add stirred for 15 min to obtain a well-mixed CDs@BiL-Eu-MOF suspension. Then, different concentrations of Ag+ solution was added to 1 mL of the above mixture and mixed thoroughly. Afterward by 1 min, images were taken (using smartphone) for the mixture solutions at room temperature reading RGB value of different color images under 365 nm UV lamp. For Cys detection; 10 mg BiL-Eu-MOF was dispersed in 10 mL DI-water and 0.4 mL of CDs was added then stirred for 15 min to obtain a well-mixed CDs@BiL-Eu-MOF suspension, Solution of 0.1 mM of Ag+ was added to 1 mL of the above mixture (CDs@BiL-Eu-MOF) and mixed thoroughly to quench the blue emission. Next, various concentrations of Cys was added and mixed thoroughly followed by taking images as mentioned above.

Various characterization techniques were carried out to investigate size, morphology, optoelectronics and thermal properties of BiL-Eu-MOF. TEM images of CD and the as-prepared BiL-Eu-MOF were shown in Fig. 2 a (A and B) respectively, and SEM images displayed in Fig. 1S (Fig. 1S, ESI). Fig. 2a shows that size of BiL-Eu-MOF is around 100 nm, while one can notice the very small carbon dots on the surface and/or encapsulated of BiL-Eu-MOF. The elemental analysis demonstrated that Eu, C and O elements were homogeneously distributed in the whole structure (Fig. 2S, ESI).

FT-IR spectra as shown in Fig. 2c were recorded to characterize functional groups on the surface of BiL-Eu-MOF. The characteristic asymmetric stretching vibrations and symmetric vibrations of carboxylic groups are in the 1430–1612 cm−1 range and 1374 cm−1, respectively. Moreover, the absence of stretching vibrations from C –OH (at 1280 cm−1) and C = O (1690–1730 cm−1) bands from free organic linker molecules indicate that the Eu atoms have been successfully coordinated with the carboxylic groups [92].

The XRD patterns of the synthesized BiL-Eu-MOF and CDs@BiL-Eu-MOF were recorded as shown in Fig. 2c, which indicated that the addition of CDs had a little effect on the main framework structure of BiL-Eu-MOF.

Thermal profile for BiL-Eu-MOF was performed using thermal gravimetry analysis. TGA curve of the BiL-Eu-MOF showed very high stability as compared to the mono-ligand Eu-MOF [92]. During thermal scanning three major weight loss processes were realized as illustrated in Fig. 2d. At the beginning until 500 °C there is no a noteworthy weight loss, then the first weight loss step could be attributed to the loss of small amount water and methanol adsorbed on the surface and in the cavities of BiL-Eu-MOF nanostructure at about 650 °C (28.49%) [91]. The second step occurred in the range of 650–800 °C, which was assigned to the partial decomposition of the organic ligand in the cavities of BiL-Eu-MOF [91]. The third step which is not a noticeable change of the weight loss that stood for the skeleton structure collapse of BiL-Eu-MOF approximately between ∼ 800–900 °C (4.3%). These results indicated the prepared BiL-Eu-MOF possessed a relatively very high thermal stability.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the BiL-Eu-MOF and CDs@BiL-Eu-MOF were shown in Fig. 2e,f. At the excitation wavelength of 280 nm, CDs solution exhibited a strong emission peak at ∼ 430 nm, and showed a strong blue fluorescence under the ultraviolet lamp (λ = 365 nm). As shown in Fig. 2e, CDs@ BiL-Eu-MOF fluorescence probe presented a strong emission peak from CDs at ∼ 430 nm, with characteristic emission peaks for the BiL-Eu-MOF. Fig. 2f shows 5 emission peaks for Eu3+ in the BiL-Eu-MOF, and as reported in literature the emission peaks were noticed. Peaks at 581, 592, 618, 656 and 700 nm, which are attributed to the transitions from 5D0 → 7F0, 5D0 → 7F1, 5D0 → 7F2, 5D0 → 7F3, 5D0 → 7F4, respectively [93]. Furthermore, visual emission of CDs@BiL-Eu-MOF suspension was observed even under 365 nm UV, this is due to the antenna effect which is from the transition of charge from the ligand, 1,10 phenanthroline, to the Eu3+ metal ions [91].

The mechanism of quenching of blue-purple emission of CDs@BiL-Eu-MOF by silver ions and then restoring of the blue-purple emission of the probe after addition Cys is suggested as follows. The blue emission of encapsulated CDs in the probe CDs@BiL-Eu-MOF was quenched after addition of silver ions and the red emission form BiL-Eu-MOF remained unchanged and served as in internal reference. Regarding the mechanism of quenching, we assume Ag+ would easily complex with sulfhydryl/amino groups on surfaces of CDs in the mixture solution of CDs and Ag+ [90,94]. Later, Cys can complex with Ag+ ions and restore the blue emission of CDs.

Selectivity of the BiL-Eu-MOF was investigated by the addition of common ions (Zn2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Na+, Cr3+, Mg2+, K+, Ca2+, Ag+ and Co2+, NO3 –, Cl-, SO4 2-), common amino acids (Tyr, His, Lys, Ser, Trp, Glut, Asp, Cys), common saccharides (Gluc, Fru, Suc), VB1, UA and AA. As shown in Fig. 3 a, under the identical conditions, no noticeable change of the emission intensity of the BiL-Eu-MOF in the presence of 0.01 M of the substances were observed, and also the fluorescence intensity was measured as shown in Fig. 3S, ESI. Therefore, the BiL-Eu-MOF was used as a reference for detection of Ag+ and Cys. The presence of two ligands (biligand) offers the irresponsive properties towards common cations, anions, and molecules (Fig. 3a). As notice, emission of Eu-MOF with BTC (Eu-MOF 1) quenches with five species (Fe3+, Tyr, Trp, VB1 and AA). Whereas, Eu-MOF coupled with 1,10 phen (Eu-MOF 2) has a very low emission and quenches with four species as well (Fe3+, Mg2+, VB1 and Cys) (Fig. 3a). However, our bi-ligand design of BiL-Eu-MOF offered a new property of being unaffected by most of the metal ions and molecules.

The novel BiL-Eu-MOF displayed some unique properties. At first, shelf-life, stability under ambient conditions, the fluorescence sensor in water and methanol was explored because stability of MOFs in solvents are very important property of MOFs. It was obvious that the fluorescence of the BiL-Eu-MOF almost stable after being stored for about six months in our laboratory under ambient condition (from −10 °C in winter to 35 °C spring) as shown in Fig. 3b. Recovery of the original signal or reusability of the bi-ligand MOF was investigated because they are important characteristics of the sensors showing their robustness and cost-effective merits. The fluorescence intensity of the BiL-Eu-MOF has almost stable even for ten times (Fig. 3c), which indicates that the bi-ligand sensor possesses a high reusability. Furthermore, the pH effect of the BiL-Eu-MOF was also studied, the fluorescence intensity hardly changed in the wide range of pH (1 to 13), which indicated that the BiL-Eu-MOF could be applied in acidic and alkaline environments (Fig. 3d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing of Ag+ and Cys
ce:section.ce:para: Using conventional fluorimetry, CD@BiL-Eu-MOF was used for detection of Ag+ ions directly and Cys indirectly. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity of the blue CDs at 420 nm of CD@BiL-Eu-MOF gradually decreased with addition of different concentrations of Ag+ ions, whereas the red emission of BiL-Eu-MOF remained almost constant and served as an internal reference. The fluorescence intensity was recorded under an excitation wavelength of 280 nm after reacting for 1 min. The ratio of the emission intensities is proportional to concentration of Ag+ ions. A dynamic linear detection range of CD@BiL-Eu-MOF towards Ag+ was measured from 0 to 40 μM (Fig. 4b) and 1.8 μM as LOD was recorded (when S/N = 3). In addition, fluorescence responses of CDs@BiL-Eu-MOF toward other metal ions and anions investigated as shown in Fig. 4S, ESI.

For Cys detection, As depicted in Fig. 4c, the emission intensity of the blue-purple emission of CD@BiL-Eu-MOF gradually increased with addition of different concentrations of Cys, due to the reaction of Cys with CD@BiL-Eu-MOF/Ag+. The fluorescence intensity was recorded under excitation wavelength of 280 nm after reacting for 1 min. A dynamic linear detection range of CD@BiL-Eu-MOF/Ag+ towards Cys was measured from 0 to 46 μM (Fig. 4d), LOD at 0.11 μM was recorded (when S/N = 3). In addition, fluorescence responses of CDs@BiL-Eu-MOF toward other metal ions and anions investigated as shown in Fig. 5 S, ESI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual sensing of Ag+ and Cys
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, a multicolor visual probe was designed for the simple and smart phone-based visual detection of Ag+ ions. With the increase of Ag+ concentration, the visual probe showed an obvious color change from purple-blue to red that can be seen by naked eyes under the UV lamp irradiation (365 nm) as shown in Fig. 5a, and photos of cells were taken by a smartphone and their RGB value were measured by Image-J software. A dynamic linear detection range of visual probe towards Ag+ was measured from 0 to 660 μM, and LOD at 2.1 μM was recorded (when S/N = 3) (Fig. 5b). As conclusion and comparing with literature, the present sensor for Ag+ detection showed a reasonable wide detection range, and fair rapid response (Table 1 ). The multicolor visual ratiometry showed a good multicolor variation from purple-blue to red as shown in Fig. 5g. Purple-blue, purple, magenta, pink and red can be noticed easily. Although for accurate quantification integration of smartphone with RGB analysis is needed, while for semi-quantitative analysis, just color estimation by naked eye might be satisfactory. In addition, the selectivity of visual sensor toward other metal ions and anions investigated as shown in Fig. 5e.

For Cys detection. With the increase of Cys concentration, the visual probe showed an obvious color change from red to purple-blue under the UV lamp irradiation (365 nm) as shown in Fig. 5c. Furthermore, the selectivity of the visual sensor was also studied. It can be seen that only Cys can change the fluorescent color obviously (Fig. 5f). These results indicate that the prepared visual sensor can be used for the facile and rapid visual detection of Cys. A dynamic linear detection range of visual probe towards Cys was measured from 0 to 405 μM. LOD at 0.15 μM was recorded (Fig. 5d).
Additionally, as shown in Table 1, compared with other previously reported methods for Cys detection, our sensor shows a wider detection range, and rapid response time.

In order to apply the CD@BiL-Eu-MOF as fluorescence probe, local river water (Khabir river in Zakho city/ Iraq) was used as a real sample for detection of silver ions and human serum samples for Cys detection. Spike recovery was used to validate the accuracy of proposed probe.

As shown in Table 2 , the spike recovery for Ag+ was found to be between 94.5% and 97.6% with RSD less than 1.2. These results suggest the potential application of the CD@BiL-Eu-MOF-based fluorescent probe for the detection of Ag+ ions in the river samples. Whereas the recovery for the detection of Cys in human serum samples was between 94.6% and 96.3% with RSD less than 0.83% as shown in Table 2. It can be concluded that the CD@BiL-Eu-MOF/Ag+-based sensing platform for Cys demonstrates high sensitivity and selectivity in a complicated biological matrix such as human serum.

A novel multicolor visual sensor based on the encapsulation of blue emissive CDs into stable and red BiL-Eu-MOF structure was designed successfully for the detection of Ag+ and Cys. The BiL-Eu-MOF not only maintained the excellent optical properties of CDs and Eu3+, but also showed excellent structural and fluorescence stability in aqueous and organic solutions. More importantly, the ratiometric fluorescence probe based on CDs@BiL-Eu-MOF could be applied to the detections of Ag+ and Cys in actual samples. Furthermore, the ability of sensor for visual detection of Cys and Ag+, make it possible to carry out point of care testing. The proposed method exhibited an excellent analytical performance as compared to traditional single emission methods or MOFs with mono-ligand. Furthermore, this work also provides a good demonstration of ratiometric visual analysis methods in further application. It is believed that this research work has opened a novel avenue for the point of care detection of Cys in the biological samples, and Ag+ ions in environmental and food samples as well.
Preparation of highly stable bi-ligand Eu-based metal organic frameworks that used as a reference signal in fluorescence ratiometry.
